---
title: "Main Review: MSstats+ Benchmark Interpretation"
subtitle: "Reviewer summary of the sample-swap benchmark concern"
author: "Witold Wolski"
date: today
format:
  pdf:
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    keep-tex: false
  html:
    self-contained: true
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
---

*Conflict of interest: I am a developer of the `prolfqua` package, one of
the comparator workflows discussed below and listed in the supplement.*

Dear authors,

Thank you for making the MSstats+ benchmark scripts and reanalysis material
available. I focused my review on the quantitative benchmark because Table 1 is
central to the manuscript's claim that MSstats+ improves differential-abundance
analysis relative to established workflows.

My concern is not that MSstats+ is implemented incorrectly. My concern is that
the K562/CSF sample-swap benchmark induces a label-dependent variance structure.
The benchmark starts from a dilution experiment in which essentially all
quantified proteins are differentially abundant. The manuscript then creates a
synthetic null class by reassigning condition labels for most proteins while
leaving a minority unchanged as Positives. This is a useful stress-test
construction for quality-aware workflows, but it is not a neutral benchmark for
general differential-abundance modelling.

In the CSF benchmark, the synthetic null proteins are constructed as
within-condition mixtures of dilution levels, whereas the Positive proteins
retain the original dilution comparison. The ground-truth label thus becomes
coupled to within-condition variance: Negatives carry inflated within-condition
variance from the dilution mixture, while Positives remain relatively clean.
Several comparator workflows use empirical-Bayes or related variance
moderation, so a shared variance prior estimated from this class-imbalanced
mixture can be pulled toward the Negative class and reduce sensitivity for
the lower-variance Positive class. Table 1 therefore partly measures how each
method responds to the sample-swap-induced variance asymmetry, and supports a
narrower conclusion than currently stated: MSstats+ performs well on this
specific sample-swap benchmark, but the table alone does not establish general
superiority over established variance-moderated workflows in standard
differential-abundance settings.

One might object that this variance coupling only reflects the natural
behaviour of poor-quality runs, which is exactly what MSstats+ is designed to
handle. That defence applies to the `all_data` benchmark, which mixes Neat,
1:2, and higher-dilution Bad runs. It does not explain the `good_data`
benchmark, which is restricted to clean Neat and 1:2 runs. The supplement
variance figures on `good_data` show the same label-linked asymmetry, so the
effect arises from the swap construction itself, not from including degraded
runs.

P-value distributions on the CSF `all_data` benchmark are non-uniform across
methods, which is additional diagnostic evidence; see supplement §3.

I recommend that the manuscript explicitly separate these claims. The
K562/CSF benchmark can be presented as a stress test for longitudinal quality
degradation and run-level anomalies. Broader claims about differential-abundance
method performance should either be qualified or supported by additional
sensitivity analyses where truth labels are not coupled to within-condition
variance, and where comparator methods can be run with appropriate
normalization and best-practice workflows without changing the estimand created
by the benchmark.

I also recommend that the main text clearly distinguish the nominal-p-value and
adjusted-p-value operating points. I understand that nominal p-values can be
useful for inspecting signal-to-noise properties, and the manuscript states that
adjusted results are provided separately. However, Table 1 is prominent and is
easy to read as a discovery-performance table. Conclusions about detected
proteins should therefore be interpreted together with FDR-controlled results in
the main benchmark discussion.

Finally, I recommend separating benchmark generation from tool-specific
modelling scripts in the public archive. A benchmark intended to compare
methods should be written to disk as fixed report, annotation, and truth files,
and all methods should consume the same fixed benchmark object. This would make
the sample-swap construction auditable and would reduce the risk that
method-specific code paths also encode parts of the benchmark generation.

I provide a longer reproducible supplement with the supporting diagnostics. It
includes the reproduced CSF Spectronaut benchmark tables, p-value distributions,
per-protein effect-size and within-condition variance diagnostics, rebuilt
sample-swap checks, and protein-swap sensitivity analyses.
Section 7 of the supplement lists the concrete points I recommend the authors
address in revision.
The rebuilt sample-swap and protein-swap analyses are not proposed as definitive
replacement benchmarks. They are sensitivity checks showing that method rankings
are sensitive to the benchmark construction and that the current Table 1 should
be interpreted with this limitation in mind.

In summary, the Table 1 ranking depends on a benchmark construction that
couples ground-truth class to within-condition variance; broader claims about
general method performance should be supported by constructions where this
coupling is absent.
